name: Frontend Architecture
description: Monorepos, micro-frontends, module patterns, and how large-scale frontend apps are structured.

cards.front: What is a monorepo and why would you use one?
cards.back:
A monorepo is a single repository containing multiple projects/packages.

• Shared code — reuse utils, types, and components across apps
• Atomic changes — update a library + all consumers in one PR
• Unified CI/CD — one pipeline config, consistent tooling
• Easier refactoring — rename across packages with confidence

Tradeoffs:
• Repo size grows fast (clone times, CI pressure)
• Needs specialized tooling (Nx, Turborepo) to stay fast
• Team boundaries can blur — ownership gets murky

Use when: multiple related apps share code (e.g., dashboard + marketing site + shared UI lib).

cards.front: Compare monorepo tools: Nx vs Turborepo vs Lerna
cards.back:
Nx:
• Full-featured build system with dependency graph
• Computation caching (local + remote)
• Code generators, plugins for React/Angular/Node
• Best for: large teams wanting structure + guardrails

Turborepo:
• Lightweight, fast task runner by Vercel
• Remote caching out of the box
• Minimal config — just turbo.json
• Best for: teams wanting speed with low setup cost

Lerna:
• OG monorepo tool, now maintained by Nx
• Focused on versioning + publishing npm packages
• Best for: open-source libraries shipping to npm

Key insight: Nx and Turborepo handle task orchestration. Lerna handles package publishing. Many teams combine them.

Micro-frontends split a single UI into independently deployable pieces, each owned by a different team.

• Each "micro-app" has its own repo, build, and deploy pipeline
• They compose at runtime in a shell/host application
• Teams pick their own frameworks (React, Vue, etc.)

Composition strategies:
• Build-time — npm packages composed during build
• Runtime — Module Federation (webpack 5), iframes, or Web Components
• Server-side — edge/server stitches HTML fragments together

Real example: An e-commerce site where the product team owns the catalog UI, the checkout team owns the cart, and a platform team owns the shell/nav.

cards.front: When do micro-frontends make sense vs when are they overkill?
cards.back:
✅ Good fit when:
• 3+ teams need to deploy independently
• Teams have different release cadences
• Legacy migration — wrap old app, build new pieces separately
• App is truly massive (100+ routes, distinct domains)

❌ Overkill when:
• Single team owns the whole frontend
• App is small-to-medium (<50 routes)
• You just want code splitting (use lazy loading instead)
• Teams share the same tech stack and release together

Hidden costs:
• Shared state is painful (event bus, URL params, or global store)
• Duplicate dependencies inflate bundle size
• Consistent UX requires a shared design system
• Debugging across boundaries is harder

Rule of thumb: start with a well-structured monolith. Split only when team autonomy demands it.

cards.front: Explain module patterns: barrel files and feature folders
cards.back:
Barrel files (index.ts):
• Re-export public API from a folder
• Example: components/index.ts exports Button, Modal, Card
• Benefit: clean imports — import { Button } from '@/components'
• Risk: can cause circular deps and tree-shaking issues if overused

Feature folders:
• Group by feature, not by type
• Example: features/auth/ has its own components/, hooks/, api/, types/
• Each feature is a self-contained module
• Reduces cross-feature coupling

Anti-pattern: grouping ALL components in /components, ALL hooks in /hooks — this scales poorly and creates a junk-drawer effect.

Senior tip: barrel files at the feature boundary, not inside every subfolder. Keep the public surface small.

cards.front: How should you structure a shared component library?
cards.back:
Recommended structure:
• packages/ui/ — the component library package
• Each component in its own folder: Button/Button.tsx, Button.test.tsx, Button.stories.tsx, index.ts
• A root index.ts barrel for public exports

Key principles:
• Headless logic vs styled components — separate behavior from visuals
• Tokens > hardcoded styles — use design tokens for colors, spacing
• Peer dependencies — don't bundle React; let consumers provide it
• Versioning strategy — semver + changelog for breaking changes

Tooling:
• Storybook for visual dev + documentation
• Chromatic or Percy for visual regression testing
• Changesets for automated versioning + changelogs

Anti-patterns:
• God components with 30+ props — split them
• Tightly coupling to a specific app's business logic

cards.front: Compare package managers: npm vs yarn vs pnpm
cards.back:
npm:
• Ships with Node — zero setup
• Flat node_modules by default
• Slower installs, larger disk usage
• Best for: small projects, quick prototypes

yarn (Classic v1 / Berry v3+):
• Deterministic installs via lockfile
• Workspaces for monorepos built in
• Berry (v3+) uses Plug'n'Play — no node_modules
• Best for: teams already invested in yarn ecosystem

pnpm:
• Content-addressable store — saves massive disk space
• Strict by default — prevents phantom dependencies
• Fastest installs in benchmarks
• Native workspace support
• Best for: monorepos, CI speed, disk-constrained envs

Senior take: pnpm is the strongest choice for new projects today. Its strictness catches real bugs (phantom deps) that npm/yarn silently allow.

cards.front: How do you handle dependency management and version conflicts in large apps?
cards.back:
Common problems:
• Version mismatch — two packages need different React versions
• Phantom dependencies — code imports a package it didn't declare
• Duplicate bundles — same lib bundled multiple times

Strategies:
• Lockfiles — always commit package-lock.json / pnpm-lock.yaml
• Renovate or Dependabot — automate dependency updates with PRs
• pnpm strict mode — catches undeclared deps at install time
• Resolutions/overrides — force a single version across the tree
• Bundle analysis — use webpack-bundle-analyzer or source-map-explorer

Monorepo-specific:
• Hoist shared deps to root (npm/yarn workspaces)
• Pin versions in a shared catalog (pnpm catalogs)
• Use a single version policy — all packages on same React version

Rule: update deps regularly in small batches, not once a year in a panic.

cards.front: What does design system architecture look like?
cards.back:
Layers of a design system:

1. Design tokens — primitive values (colors, spacing, radii, shadows)
2. Core components — Button, Input, Modal, Typography
3. Composite patterns — SearchBar, DataTable, FormField
4. Page templates — layouts and page-level patterns

Architecture decisions:
• Token format: use Style Dictionary or Figma Tokens to generate CSS vars / JS objects
• Theming: CSS custom properties for runtime theme switching
• Docs: Storybook as the single source of truth
• Versioning: semver the package; breaking changes = major bump

Org model:
• Centralized — one team owns the system (scales well, slower to add)
• Federated — contributors from product teams (faster, needs governance)
• Hybrid — core team reviews, product teams propose

Success metric: product teams reach for the design system first, not custom CSS.

cards.front: How do you manage environment configuration (.env, feature flags, config layers)?
cards.back:
Config layers (from lowest to highest priority):
1. Defaults in code — sensible fallbacks
2. .env files — .env.development, .env.production, .env.local
3. CI/CD variables — injected at build time
4. Runtime config — fetched from API at startup

.env best practices:
• Never commit secrets — use .env.local + .gitignore
• Prefix with VITE_ or NEXT_PUBLIC_ to expose to client
• Validate at startup — fail fast if required vars are missing

Feature flags:
• Simple: JSON config or env vars for on/off toggles
• Advanced: LaunchDarkly, Unleash, Statsig for gradual rollouts
• Use flags to decouple deploy from release
• Clean up stale flags — they become tech debt fast

Senior tip: treat config as a contract. Type it, validate it, and document what each value does.

cards.front: Compare rendering patterns: CSR vs SSR vs SSG vs ISR
cards.back:
CSR (Client-Side Rendering):
• Browser downloads empty HTML, JS renders everything
• Fast transitions, poor initial load + SEO
• Use for: dashboards, internal tools, SPAs behind auth

SSR (Server-Side Rendering):
• Server renders HTML per request, sends it to browser
• Great SEO + fast first paint, higher server cost
• Use for: dynamic content, personalized pages

SSG (Static Site Generation):
• HTML generated at build time
• Blazing fast, served from CDN, stale until rebuild
• Use for: blogs, docs, marketing sites

ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration):
• SSG + background revalidation after a timeout
• Best of both — fast CDN delivery + fresh data
• Use for: e-commerce product pages, content sites with frequent updates

Key: pick per-route, not per-app. Next.js lets you mix all four.

cards.front: When should you use Next.js vs plain React with Vite?
cards.back:
Choose Next.js when:
• You need SSR, SSG, or ISR
• SEO matters (marketing, e-commerce, content)
• You want file-based routing out of the box
• You need API routes (lightweight BFF)
• Image optimization, middleware, edge functions

Choose React + Vite when:
• Pure SPA — dashboard, admin panel, internal tool
• No server-side rendering needed
• You want minimal abstraction and full control
• Faster dev server startup (Vite is very fast)
• Deploying to static hosting (S3, Netlify, Vercel static)

Tradeoffs:
• Next.js adds complexity — server components, caching rules, deploy constraints
• Vite is simpler but you wire up routing, data fetching, SSR yourself

Senior take: don't adopt Next.js just because it's popular. If your app is behind a login and SEO is irrelevant, Vite + React Router is simpler and faster to iterate on.

cards.front: What is the BFF (Backend For Frontend) pattern?
cards.back:
A BFF is a backend service tailored specifically for one frontend's needs.

• Sits between the frontend and backend microservices
• Aggregates data from multiple APIs into one response
• Shapes data exactly how the UI needs it
• Handles auth, caching, and error formatting for the client

Why use it:
• Frontend stops doing 5 API calls to render one page
• Backend teams don't bloat their APIs with UI-specific fields
• Different frontends (web, mobile) can have different BFFs

Implementation options:
• Next.js API routes / Route Handlers
• Express or Fastify service
• GraphQL gateway (acts as a natural BFF)
• Edge functions for low-latency aggregation

Anti-pattern: putting business logic in the BFF — it should orchestrate and transform, not own domain rules.

cards.front: What are the best folder structure strategies for large frontend apps?
cards.back:
Strategy 1 — Feature-based (recommended):
• src/features/auth/, src/features/dashboard/, src/features/settings/
• Each feature owns its components, hooks, api, types, tests
• Shared code in src/shared/ or src/common/

Strategy 2 — Domain-driven:
• src/domains/user/, src/domains/billing/, src/domains/catalog/
• Maps to business domains, great for DDD-aligned teams

Strategy 3 — Layer-based (avoid at scale):
• src/components/, src/hooks/, src/utils/, src/services/
• Fine for small apps, becomes a junk drawer at 50+ files per folder

Rules that scale:
• Colocation — test files next to source files
• Barrel exports at feature boundary only
• Enforce dependency rules — features/ can't import across features directly; use shared/
• Keep it flat — avoid nesting beyond 3 levels

Senior tip: the best structure mirrors your team structure (Conway's Law).

cards.front: How do you identify and manage technical debt in a frontend codebase?
cards.back:
Identifying tech debt:
• Slow CI — builds/tests taking 10+ minutes
• Fear of changing code — "don't touch that file"
• Duplicate logic — same fetch/transform in 5 places
• Outdated dependencies — 2+ major versions behind
• No types — any/unknown scattered everywhere
• Dead code — unused components, feature-flagged-off experiments

Managing it:
• Track it — use a tech-debt backlog or ADRs (Architecture Decision Records)
• Budget time — 10-20% of each sprint for debt paydown
• Boy Scout Rule — leave code cleaner than you found it
• Strangler pattern — wrap old code, build new beside it, migrate gradually
• Lint rules as guardrails — eslint-plugin-deprecation, no-restricted-imports

Prioritize by:
• Blast radius — how much code does this affect?
• Developer pain — is this slowing the team daily?
• Risk — could this cause a production incident?

Senior tip: tech debt isn't bad code. It's a strategic tradeoff. Document WHY the shortcut was taken.
